page.title: The Assembly
page.description: Component architecture: reliable building blocks designed around explicit contracts — clear responsibilities, predictable states, composable structures, and behavior that can be tested in isolation.

hero.title: The Assembly
hero.subtitle: Component Architecture
hero.lead: Components are where product intent becomes an executable interface.
hero.intro: I design component architectures around explicit contracts: clear responsibilities, predictable states, composable structures, and behavior that can be tested in isolation. The goal is not to produce more reusable components. It is to create reliable building blocks that remain understandable as products, requirements, and teams evolve.

metadata.focus: Component APIs, composition, state modeling, testing, and accessibility
metadata.scope: Frontend components used across product features and shared interface systems
metadata.impact: Safer reuse, clearer implementation decisions, and more maintainable interfaces

labels.core: Component architecture: contracts, boundaries, composition, state, and quality at the component boundary
labels.impact: The impact of treating components as architectural contracts

primaryContent.title: Contracts Before Implementation
paragraphs: A component is an agreement between the system that provides it and the product code that consumes it.

Before implementation, I clarify what the component owns, what callers control, and which assumptions should remain internal. Inputs should communicate intent rather than expose implementation details. Outputs should be deliberate. Defaults should support the common case without making exceptional cases impossible.
A useful component contract answers four questions:

items: What responsibility belongs to the component?
What responsibility remains with the consumer?
How can the contract evolve without creating unnecessary breakage?

closingParagraphs: Clear contracts reduce interpretation. They also make reviews more productive because decisions can be evaluated against defined responsibilities rather than personal preference.

diagram.title: The Component Contract
diagram.description: Shows the responsibilities exchanged between a component and its consumer.
diagram.caption: A stable component hides implementation decisions while making supported behavior explicit.
diagram.summary: A left-to-right flow: Consumer Intent passes through the Public API into Component Behavior, which produces an Observable Result.

stages.name: Consumer Intent
stages.note: What the caller is trying to express.

stages.name: Public API
stages.note: The stable surface that communicates supported usage.

stages.name: Observable Result
stages.note: The deliberate output callers can rely on.

primaryContent.title: Atomic Design as a Baseline
paragraphs: Atomic Design provides a useful vocabulary for organizing component systems, but I do not treat it as a rigid hierarchy or an architectural rule.

Its value is in making levels of reuse visible, from foundational decisions to complete product experiences. The model helps classify what is being built, while component contracts, composition, accessibility, ownership, and real product constraints determine how each part should behave and scale.

items.title: Foundations
items.description: Design tokens, semantic conventions, interaction principles, and implementation guidelines that establish shared constraints.

items.title: Atoms
items.description: Focused interface primitives such as buttons, inputs, icons, and labels.

items.title: Molecules
items.description: Small combinations of primitives that perform a recognizable interaction or communicate a specific piece of information.

items.title: Organisms
items.description: Self-contained interface sections that coordinate multiple components around a broader product responsibility.

items.title: Templates
items.description: Page-level structures that define hierarchy, regions, and composition without owning every product behavior.

items.title: Product Experiences
items.description: Real product surfaces where components encounter data, workflows, edge cases, accessibility requirements, and operational constraints.

closingParagraphs: The relationship is not only bottom-up. Product experiences expose missing states, weak assumptions, and recurring needs that should inform component contracts and, when appropriate, improve the foundation layer.

diagram.title: Atomic Design Feedback Model
diagram.description: Shows how levels of reuse move from shared foundations into real products and return as system feedback.
diagram.caption: Atomic Design organizes levels of reuse; contracts, composition, and product feedback make them scalable.
diagram.summary: Foundations feed Atoms, which combine into Molecules, then Organisms, then Templates, and finally Product Experiences. Product experiences return Foundation Feedback that informs the foundation layer.

stages.name: Foundation Feedback
stages.note: Missing states and needs loop back.

primaryContent.closingStatement: Atomic Design organizes the system conceptually. Component architecture determines whether that system remains usable under change.

primaryContent.title: Boundaries That Protect Change
paragraphs: Good boundaries prevent a local decision from spreading through the interface.

I separate presentation, interaction behavior, product state, and external data concerns when they change for different reasons. A component should not quietly become responsible for API orchestration, business workflows, global state, and visual rendering at the same time.

The appropriate boundary depends on context. A product-specific component may reasonably contain more behavior than a shared primitive. A component intended for broad reuse requires tighter constraints, fewer assumptions, and a more stable public surface.
primaryContent.closingStatement: The objective is not maximum abstraction. It is to place the boundary where change can remain understandable and contained.

primaryContent.title: Composition Over Configuration
paragraphs: Configuration is useful until a component attempts to describe every possible product requirement through an expanding set of properties.

I prefer composition when consumers need meaningful structural control. Slots, child components, controlled regions, and explicit extension points allow variation without turning one component into a conditional system.
Configuration remains appropriate for finite choices such as size, emphasis, orientation, or interaction mode. Composition is stronger when content hierarchy or behavior must differ.
The decision depends on the type of variability:

items: Use separate components when responsibilities no longer belong together.
Avoid extension points that bypass the original contract.

closingParagraphs: This keeps component APIs smaller while allowing product teams to assemble complex experiences from understandable parts.

diagram.title: Choosing the Extension Model
diagram.description: Clarifies when variability should be handled through configuration, composition, or separation.
diagram.caption: The type of variation should determine the architecture of the extension point.
diagram.summary: A required variation is classified as a bounded choice, a structural difference, or a new responsibility. A bounded choice becomes configuration, a structural difference becomes composition, and a new responsibility becomes a separate component.

stages.name: Required Variation
stages.note: Start by naming the kind of variability.

primaryContent.title: States Are Part of the Architecture
paragraphs: A component is not complete when only its default state works.

Loading, empty, disabled, invalid, selected, expanded, partial, and error states affect behavior, accessibility, testing, and product expectations. I model these states intentionally rather than allowing them to emerge from unrelated flags and conditional rendering.

When several properties can create contradictory combinations, the API is usually exposing implementation mechanics instead of valid states. In those cases, explicit variants, state models, or separate components can make the contract safer.
State design should define:
closingParagraphs: Treating states as architecture prevents visual correctness from hiding behavioral ambiguity.

diagram.title: Component Quality Loop
diagram.description: Connects implementation decisions to verification and continued use.
diagram.caption: Component quality is maintained through repeated validation of the public contract, not only through the initial implementation.
diagram.summary: A loop moving from Contract to States to Interaction to Accessibility and Tests to Consumer Feedback, and finally to Contract Refinement, which returns to the contract.

primaryContent.title: Quality at the Component Boundary
paragraphs: Testing and accessibility are part of the contract, not final-stage verification.

I test observable behavior: rendered output, interaction, state transitions, emitted events, keyboard support, and failure conditions. Tests should protect important guarantees without reproducing internal implementation line by line.

Accessibility receives the same treatment. Semantic structure, accessible names, keyboard interaction, focus order, status communication, and error association must remain correct across supported states and compositions.

Automated checks provide a baseline, but they do not replace architectural judgment. A component can pass static checks while still creating confusing navigation, hidden state, or an unusable interaction model.
primaryContent.closingStatement: The component boundary is where these guarantees can be established once and preserved across repeated use.

items.title: Reduced Ambiguity
items.description: Explicit responsibilities and state models give engineers a shared interpretation of how a component should behave.

items.title: Safer Change
items.description: Contained boundaries allow internal implementation to evolve without forcing unnecessary changes across consuming products.

items.title: More Effective Reuse
items.description: Purposeful APIs and composition points support recurring product needs without accumulating unrelated options.

items.title: Stronger Quality
items.description: Testing and accessibility requirements become repeatable guarantees rather than responsibilities recreated by every implementation team.

items.title: Better Engineering Decisions
items.description: Teams spend less time debating local conventions and more time evaluating behavior, constraints, and product-specific trade-offs.

items.title: The Pattern
items.description: Design systems provide the governance, shared language, and distribution model through which component architecture can scale across teams.

items.title: The Map
items.description: Product architecture determines where shared components end and where feature state, data flow, and application responsibilities begin.

items.title: The Fixer
items.description: Diagnosis and prevention connect to component work when unclear ownership, invalid states, or fragile contracts create recurring defects.

items.title: Thomson Reuters
items.description: Current product and design-system work provides practical evidence of extending components, defining integration boundaries, improving accessibility, and supporting adoption across product contexts.

closingThought.statement: A component becomes architectural when its contract makes change easier to understand, contain, and verify.
